verses.text: The children of Israel committed a trespass in the accursed thing: for Achan, son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, took of the accursed thing: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against the children of Israel. Guidance: One man pockets, the whole nation sins. The narrator gives us what no spy could see: the army does not know yet, Joshua does not know yet, but God knows. The full genealogy is read out — sin is never anonymous in scripture. And the verb 'kindled' tells us the next chapter will be a fire before it is anything else.

Joshua sent men from Jericho to Ai, which is beside Beth-aven, east of Beth-el: Go up and view the country. The men went up and viewed Ai. They returned: Let not all the people go up; but let about two or three thousand men go up and smite Ai; make not all the people to labour thither; for they are but few. There went up about three thousand men: they fled before the men of Ai. The men of Ai smote about thirty and six men: they chased them from before the gate unto Shebarim, smote them in the going down: wherefore the hearts of the people melted, and became as water. Guidance: After the seven-day procession at Jericho, this is a one-day raid. Confidence has shifted from God to ground-level analysis: 'they are but few.' Three thousand men go without prayer. Thirty-six die — small numerically, devastating spiritually. The same hearts that the LORD melted in Canaan now melt in Israel. The reverse miracle is just as exact as the original.

Joshua rent his clothes, fell to the earth upon his face before the ark of the LORD until the eventide, he and the elders of Israel, put dust upon their heads. Joshua said, Alas, O Lord GOD, wherefore hast thou at all brought this people over Jordan, to deliver us into the hand of the Amorites, to destroy us? would to God we had been content, and dwelt on the other side Jordan! O Lord, what shall I say, when Israel turneth their backs before their enemies! For the Canaanites and all the inhabitants of the land shall hear of it, and shall environ us round, and cut off our name from the earth: and what wilt thou do unto thy great name? Guidance: Joshua sounds like Moses at his worst moments — 'would to God we had stayed across the Jordan.' But notice where the prayer ends: not at Israel's reputation, but at God's. 'What wilt thou do unto thy great name?' That is intercession at its best. Even in a confused lament, the heart is in the right place: the LORD's glory is what matters.

The LORD said unto Joshua, Get thee up; wherefore liest thou thus upon thy face? Israel hath sinned, they have also transgressed my covenant which I commanded them: for they have even taken of the accursed thing, and have also stolen, and dissembled also, and they have put it even among their own stuff. Therefore the children of Israel could not stand before their enemies, but turned their backs before their enemies, because they were accursed: neither will I be with you any more, except ye destroy the accursed from among you. Guidance: There is a time for prostration and a time for action. God interrupts the prayer: this is not a problem prayer alone can fix. Sin in the camp is to be addressed, not lamented. Four verbs: taken, stolen, dissembled, put it among their own stuff. The whole moral arc of theft. And the verdict: I am not with you while the cursed thing is with you.

Up, sanctify the people, say, Sanctify yourselves against to morrow: thus saith the LORD God of Israel, There is an accursed thing in the midst of thee, O Israel: thou canst not stand before thine enemies, until ye take away the accursed thing from among you. In the morning ye shall be brought according to your tribes: the tribe the LORD taketh shall come by families; the family by households; the household man by man. He that is taken with the accursed thing shall be burnt with fire, he and all that he hath: because he hath transgressed the covenant of the LORD, and because he hath wrought folly in Israel. Guidance: God offers Achan a night. A long, silent night between the sentence and the lot, with God's finger already on his name. He could have walked to Joshua's tent, knelt, returned the goods. He did not. Repentance has an expiry. God's invitation through process is part of His grace; ignoring it forces His justice.

Joshua rose up early, brought Israel by their tribes; tribe of Judah was taken. He brought the family of Judah; the family of the Zarhites was taken. He brought the family of the Zarhites man by man; Zabdi was taken. He brought his household man by man, and Achan, son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was taken. Guidance: Four narrowing lots. Achan watched the circle close on him through the long morning — Judah first (one of twelve), then Zarhites, then Zabdi's household, then his name alone. Sin is exposed in slow motion when fast confession is refused. The lot does not 'discover' Achan; it simply ratifies what God already knew in verse 1.

Joshua said unto Achan: My son, give, I pray thee, glory to the LORD God of Israel, make confession unto him; tell me now what thou hast done; hide it not from me. Achan answered: Indeed I have sinned against the LORD God of Israel, and thus and thus have I done: When I saw among the spoils a goodly Babylonish garment, and two hundred shekels of silver, and a wedge of gold of fifty shekels weight, then I coveted them, and took them; behold, they are hid in the earth in the midst of my tent, and the silver under it. Guidance: 'My son' — Joshua speaks tenderly even now. Confession is asked as worship: 'give glory to the LORD.' Achan's three verbs are the ancient story of sin: I saw, I coveted, I took (Gen 3:6, James 1:14-15). A Babylonish robe — the spoil of empire, fashioned in idol-country. Even the choice of trophy whispers where his heart already was.

Joshua sent messengers, they ran unto the tent; behold, it was hid in his tent, and the silver under it. They took them out of the midst of the tent, brought them unto Joshua, and all the children of Israel, and laid them out before the LORD. Guidance: The messengers run. Stolen goods buried under the tent floor — exactly where Achan said. The fabric and silver are 'laid out before the LORD' as evidence. The objects that were stolen from God's treasury are returned to His presence — not for use, but for verdict. Hidden sin always ends up on display.

Joshua sent messengers, they ran unto the tent; behold, it was hid in his tent, and the silver under it. They took them out of the midst of the tent, brought them unto Joshua, and all the children of Israel, and laid them out before the LORD. Guidance: The messengers run. Stolen goods buried under the tent floor — exactly where Achan said. The fabric and silver are 'laid out before the LORD' as evidence. The objects that were stolen from God's treasury are returned to His presence — not for use, but for verdict. Hidden sin always ends up on display.

Joshua, and all Israel with him, took Achan, the silver, the garment, the wedge of gold, his sons, daughters, oxen, asses, sheep, his tent, and all he had: brought them unto the valley of Achor. Joshua said, Why hast thou troubled us? the LORD shall trouble thee this day. All Israel stoned him with stones, burned them with fire, after they had stoned them with stones. They raised over him a great heap of stones unto this day. So the LORD turned from the fierceness of his anger. Wherefore that place was called the valley of Achor, unto this day. Guidance: Achor — 'trouble.' Joshua's pun echoes Achan's own name. A whole household joins him in judgment, a hard reality the text does not soften: family was complicit, or at least knew (Babylonish robe, 200 shekels, gold wedge do not bury themselves under a tent floor unnoticed). And yet — a centuries-later prophet will speak: 'I will give her the valley of Achor for a door of hope' (Hos 2:15). The trouble valley becomes the doorway. God can redeem any address.
